common_name: Silk Floss Tree (Palo Borracho, Pink Floss Silk Tree, Ceiba speciosa)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Ceiba speciosa grows rapidly in zone 9-10 and is surprisingly cold-tolerant for a tropical-looking tree — established specimens survive temperatures to 25°F with damage to young growth. The trunk is typically covered with large, sharp conical spines, especially on young trees (the spines may diminish on older trunks). In zone 9-10, the tree typically drops its leaves in fall just before the spectacular pink bloom flush. The large seed pods release abundant white silky floss in late winter.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Silk Floss Tree is drought-tolerant once established and resents waterlogged conditions. Ceiba speciosa (formerly Chorisia speciosa) is a large, fast-growing ornamental tree native to the Gran Chaco and Atlantic Forest regions of South America (Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Paraguay, Uruguay) in the family Malvaceae (the mallow family, which also includes Hibiscus and cotton). The tree is immediately identifiable at any age by its green to gray-green trunk studded with large, conical, bottle-shaped spines that cover the bark from ground to canopy — the spines function as water-storage adaptations during the dry season of its native habitat and create a sculptural, primeval appearance. In fall (October–November in zone 9), after the leaves have dropped, the bare, spiny branches produce an extraordinary display of large (4–6 inch), 5-petaled flowers in shades of bright pink to rose-magenta with a white and yellow-streaked center — a bloom so striking on leafless branches that the tree resembles a pink-flowering cloud when viewed from a distance. The large, oval seed pods that follow split open in late winter to release abundant silky white floss that carries the seeds on the wind. Established trees are impressively cold-tolerant — a remarkable quality in a tree with so tropical an appearance.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ceiba speciosa (Silk Floss Tree)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Silk Floss Tree for Gulf Coast zone 9
LSU AgCenter — Tropical-Looking Hardy Trees for Louisiana Landscapes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Ceiba speciosa

common_name: Madagascar Palm (Spiny Club, Club-Foot, Lamerei Pachypodium)

planting.note: Plant in full sun with sandy, extremely well-drained soil; Madagascar Palm is frost-sensitive and cannot tolerate temperatures below 40°F. In zone 9, grow as a large container specimen moved inside in winter. The plant goes fully dormant in winter — it is normal for all leaves to drop; resume watering in spring when new leaves appear. Water moderately in the growing season (spring–fall); water very sparingly in winter dormancy. The trunk and stems contain a milky toxic sap — wear gloves when working near the plant. The 3 long, sharp spines at each leaf scar are painful.

directions: Plant in full sun in sandy, gravelly, or any extremely well-draining soil medium — Madagascar Palm rots rapidly in heavy or moisture-retaining soils and must never sit in standing water. Pachypodium lamerei is a tall, columnar succulent tree native to the arid regions of southern Madagascar in the family Apocynaceae (the same family as Plumeria, Oleander, and Hoya), one of the most striking and distinctive succulents regularly available in the horticultural trade. Despite the common name 'Madagascar Palm,' it is not a palm at all — it belongs to no monocot group but is instead a tropical succulent adapted to the dry, spiny deserts of Madagascar. The plant grows as a tall, single column (branching occasionally with age) of silvery-gray to greenish, ridged stem covered along its entire length with clusters of three sharp, gray-white spines 1–2 inches long at each leaf scar — the spines and the architectural, column-like form create an immediate visual impression of a cactus from the American deserts but represent an entirely convergent adaptation on another continent. From the top of the column, a rosette of long, narrow, dark-green leaves appears in the growing season. On mature specimens, the column tip produces fragrant, white, pinwheel-shaped flowers (resembling Plumeria) — rare and prized in cultivation. The milky sap is toxic. A dramatic, architectural container specimen or landscape accent for hot, dry, zone 10-11 gardens.
sources: Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Pachypodium lamerei species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pachypodium for indoor and outdoor culture
Cactus and Succulent Society of America — Pachypodium lamerei cultivation
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Pachypodium (toxic)

common_name: Rose of Venezuela (Scarlet Bouquet Tree, Brownea grandiceps)

planting.note: Plant in partial shade with consistently moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Brownea grandiceps is an understory tree from humid tropical forests and grows best with protection from intense afternoon sun and drying winds. It is frost-sensitive and best suited to zone 10-11 outdoors. The new leaves emerge as remarkable bronze-pink, limp pendant tassels that gradually stiffen and turn green — protect these emerging leaf tassels from mechanical damage. Site where the spectacular flower heads can be viewed from below or from eye level.

directions: Plant in partial shade to dappled light in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Brownea grandiceps is a natural tropical forest understory tree that grows in the bright, filtered light beneath the forest canopy and performs best in garden conditions that replicate this environment. Brownea grandiceps is a medium-sized evergreen tree from Venezuela and adjacent Colombia in the family Leguminosae (Fabaceae), considered one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the tropical world — alongside Amherstia nobilis and Delonix regia — for the extraordinary quality and appearance of its flowers. The inflorescences are large, dense, spherical or dome-shaped heads of 50–100 individual tubular flowers, each 2–3 inches long, in vivid scarlet to orange-red with pale yellow stamens — the combined head is 8–14 inches across and resembles a massive, burning, scarlet cushion or bouquet emerging directly from the branch tips. The flowers are produced in successive flushes, primarily in spring and fall, and at peak bloom an established tree may carry dozens of the massive heads simultaneously. The emerging new leaves are equally spectacular: they appear as long, limp, silky, coppery-pink tassels hanging vertically from the branch tips before gradually stiffening and turning green — a phenomenon called 'juvenile leaf wilting' that occurs in many rainforest trees as a strategy to make young leaves unattractive to herbivores. Among the most prized ornamental trees in zone 10-11 tropical collections.
sources: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Brownea grandiceps cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Brownea grandiceps species profile
Missouri Botanical Garden — Brownea species: spectacular tropical flowers
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical flowering trees for South Florida

common_name: Alexandrian Laurel (Tamanu, Beauty Leaf, Beautyleaf, Kamani)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in late spring in full sun with well-drained, sandy to moderately fertile soil; Alexandrian Laurel is extremely salt-tolerant and is one of the best large trees for coastal zone 10-11 landscapes. It is frost-sensitive but very fast-growing — young trees damaged by cold recover rapidly. The round fruits contain 'tamanu oil' (foraha oil), a golden-green, nutty-fragrant oil with significant value in cosmetics and traditional medicine throughout Polynesia and Southeast Asia.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, sandy or rocky coastal soils — Alexandrian Laurel is one of the most salt-tolerant large trees available for tropical coastal gardens and is an outstanding choice for beachfront and coastal landscapes in zone 10-11. Calophyllum inophyllum is a large, slow-to-moderately growing evergreen tree native to coastal environments from East Africa through South and Southeast Asia to Australia and the Pacific Islands — one of the few tree species with a genuinely pan-tropical coastal distribution, adapted to sandy, saline soils and salt-laden coastal winds. The tree produces a broad, spreading, dense canopy of large (4–8 inch), glossy, dark-green, oval leaves with distinctive parallel lateral veins that converge at both the midrib and leaf edge — a leaf venation pattern so distinctive that it is diagnostic for the genus Calophyllum. In spring and summer, the tree produces small clusters of intensely fragrant, white, 4-petaled flowers with prominent yellow stamens — the fragrance is sweet, fresh, and carries on the breeze. The round, green fruits (1–1.5 inches) contain a nut that is pressed to yield tamanu oil — a thick, nutty, golden-green fixed oil traditionally used throughout Polynesia, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific Islands for wound healing, skin care, and as a muscle pain remedy, and now widely used as a premium ingredient in natural cosmetics. The wood has been used for traditional outrigger canoes and structural timber throughout the Pacific.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calophyllum inophyllum for Florida landscapes
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Calophyllum inophyllum cultivation
Pacific Community — Tamanu oil: Calophyllum inophyllum in Pacific Island cultures
LSU AgCenter — Large Coastal Trees for Gulf Coast Zone 10-11

common_name: Tipu Tree (Pride of Bolivia, Rosewood of Bolivia, Tipu)

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in full sun with well-drained soil; Tipu Tree grows rapidly in zone 9-10 and can add 3–5 feet of height per year in good conditions. It is semi-evergreen to briefly deciduous in zone 9, dropping leaves in late winter before the spring leaf flush and peak bloom. The flower display occurs just after (or just before) the new leaf flush — the entire tree covered in yellow-orange flowers is a spectacular annual display. The distinctive single-winged seed pods (samaras) are ornamental. Root system is invasive — plant away from paving and structures.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil — Tipu Tree grows most vigorously in full sun and well-drained soil, becoming drought-tolerant once established. Tipuana tipu is a fast-growing, large, semi-evergreen to deciduous ornamental tree native to Bolivia, Argentina, and Brazil in the legume family (Fabaceae), monotypic in its genus. The tree has become one of the most widely planted street and shade trees in Mediterranean-climate cities worldwide (particularly in California, Spain, Argentina, South Africa, and Australia) and in zone 9-11 Gulf Coast and Florida landscapes. In late spring to early summer, the tree produces its annual bloom: an explosion of small, pea-like flowers in rich apricot-yellow to golden-orange covering the entire canopy — the display can be so dense that the foliage is temporarily obscured by the flowers, and the falling petals create a golden carpet beneath the tree. The flowers are followed by distinctive single-winged samara seed pods that spin as they fall. The broad, spreading canopy (often exceeding the tree's height in spread) provides exceptional shade on large lawns and public spaces. The compound leaves — each composed of 13–21 paired leaflets — are a fine-textured, light green that dances in the breeze. A magnificent specimen tree for large residential and public zone 9-11 landscapes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tipuana tipu for Florida landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tipu Tree for Gulf Coast zone 9-10
LSU AgCenter — Large Shade Trees for Gulf Coast Louisiana Landscapes
Floridata Plant Encyclopedia — Tipuana tipu
